details.transcribed: You'd be surprised—a lot of our users put emoji in their project names. Okay, noted. We should also look at our CI pipeline while we're at it. Build times have been creeping up—it's like 18 minutes now for a full test run. Yeah, that's annoying. It used to be like eight minutes. I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, we can add that—probably a half-day of work. I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign—big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking—I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers—we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose. ...what works for them. Right, give people options—I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views—how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in—start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with—very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this, the one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Is this the rebrand that's been going on for like six months? Yep, same one—it's finally wrapping up. Yeah, I told him, he's not thrilled. He'll get over it. What even is the orange? Like a bright orange or more muted? It's like a warm tangerine, it's actually nice. Raj just has strong opinions about everything. That's fair, gotta love a passionate designer though. Oh, definitely, his work is excellent, he just needs to pick his battles. Oh wait, sorry, I need to ask—are we changing the API responses at all for the dashboard? Because if the data shape changes, I need to redo the caching layer. Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned? No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me. then. That's the best kind of project—one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure—what's going on?

Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, we can add that—probably a half-day of work. I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign—big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking—I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers—we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose. ...what works for them. Right, give people options—I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views—how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in—start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with—very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this, the one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Is this the rebrand that's been going on for like six months? Yep, same one—it's finally wrapping up. Yeah, I told him, he's not thrilled. He'll get over it. What even is the orange? Like a bright orange or more muted? It's like a warm tangerine, it's actually nice. Raj just has strong opinions about everything. That's fair, gotta love a passionate designer though. Oh, definitely, his work is excellent, he just needs to pick his battles. Oh wait, sorry, I need to ask—are we changing the API responses at all for the dashboard? Because if the data shape changes, I need to redo the caching layer. Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned? No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me. then. That's the best kind of project—one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure—what's going on? Oh, one more design thing—the accessibility audit came back? How'd we do? Not great, honestly. Fourteen violations, most are contrast issues in the sidebar and some missing ARIA labels on the modals. How hard are those to fix? The contrast ones are easy, just color changes. The ARIA labels are a bit more work, but nothing crazy—maybe a week total. A week is nothing, let's just knock it out. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot—is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this—we lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week—it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog. That's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap. Fair, yeah, you're right. Okay, one more thing though—I ran a user research session last week with five customers. The biggest ask was better notifications settings. People feel like they get too many emails from us. I can confirm—I signed up for a test account and got like twelve emails in the first week. Yeah, it's a lot. Some people are hitting unsubscribe, which is not what we want. That's easy to fix—can we just add a preferences page? Yeah, I already mocked one up—I'll show it at the design review Wednesday. Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both—onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. It'll be fine, they're both quick—fifteen minutes each tops. Great, okay David, infrastructure—what's going on? Alright, so, first thing—the cloud bill for February came in. Boy, how bad? Brace yourselves—sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord, whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump. Twelve thousand dollars more in one month—what happened? What's driving it? Couple things. The GPU instances for the ML pipeline are expensive—that's like twenty thousand by itself now. And then somebody left three staging environments running over the weekend. That was four grand just sitting there doing nothing. Who left the staging environments up? I'm not gonna name names, but it was someone on your team, Bob. Great, I'll have a conversation with them. It happens more than you'd think—I've seen it at every company I've worked at. We can't keep doing that—four thousand dollars for a weekend. I know, I know, I'll talk to the team about it. We need to automate this, like just kill anything that's been idle. Already doing it. I'm setting up a Lambda function that terminates any staging environment that's been idle for more than four hours. Should be done by end of week. That's a great idea—no more relying on humans to remember. Honestly, everything that can be automated should be automated. Humans forget. Totally agree. Preaching to the choir, that's my whole job philosophy. Okay, so the Lambda cleanup is in progress—love it. Good, what about the GPU costs? Those aren't going away. No, but I talked to our AWS rep, Vanessa, Vanessa Park. She's putting together a reserved instance proposal that could save us about thirty percent. Thirty percent? That's significant. Yeah, yeah, thirty percent is significant—that's real money. Thirty percent off the GPU specifically? Otto, what were the accessibility audit results—how many issues? Off total compute, so we'd go from like sixty-three down to forty-five, forty-six range. That's a big saving—that would basically pay for the offsite twice. When do we get the proposal? End of this week, she said. Is that typical? Like, how fast do these proposals usually come through? For reserved instances, yeah, she's been pretty responsive. Good, I like responsive vendors—makes everything easier. Hey Otto, make a note of that—Vanessa Park, reserved instances proposal, end of this week.

My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, we can add that—probably a half-day of work. I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign—big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking—I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers—we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose. ...what works for them. Right, give people options—I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views—how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in—start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with—very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this, the one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Is this the rebrand that's been going on for like six months? Yep, same one—it's finally wrapping up. Yeah, I told him, he's not thrilled. He'll get over it. What even is the orange? Like a bright orange or more muted? It's like a warm tangerine, it's actually nice. Raj just has strong opinions about everything. That's fair, gotta love a passionate designer though. Oh, definitely, his work is excellent, he just needs to pick his battles. Oh wait, sorry, I need to ask—are we changing the API responses at all for the dashboard? Because if the data shape changes, I need to redo the caching layer. Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned? No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me. then. That's the best kind of project—one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure—what's going on? Oh, one more design thing—the accessibility audit came back? How'd we do? Not great, honestly. Fourteen violations, most are contrast issues in the sidebar and some missing ARIA labels on the modals. How hard are those to fix? The contrast ones are easy, just color changes. The ARIA labels are a bit more work, but nothing crazy—maybe a week total. A week is nothing, let's just knock it out. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot—is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this—we lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week—it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog. That's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap. Fair, yeah, you're right. Okay, one more thing though—I ran a user research session last week with five customers. The biggest ask was better notifications settings. People feel like they get too many emails from us. I can confirm—I signed up for a test account and got like twelve emails in the first week. Yeah, it's a lot. Some people are hitting unsubscribe, which is not what we want. That's easy to fix—can we just add a preferences page? Yeah, I already mocked one up—I'll show it at the design review Wednesday. Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both—onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. It'll be fine, they're both quick—fifteen minutes each tops. Great, okay David, infrastructure—what's going on? Alright, so, first thing—the cloud bill for February came in. Boy, how bad? Brace yourselves—sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord, whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump. Twelve thousand dollars more in one month—what happened? What's driving it? Couple things. The GPU instances for the ML pipeline are expensive—that's like twenty thousand by itself now. And then somebody left three staging environments running over the weekend. That was four grand just sitting there doing nothing. Who left the staging environments up? I'm not gonna name names, but it was someone on your team, Bob. Great, I'll have a conversation with them. It happens more than you'd think—I've seen it at every company I've worked at. We can't keep doing that—four thousand dollars for a weekend. I know, I know, I'll talk to the team about it. We need to automate this, like just kill anything that's been idle. Already doing it. I'm setting up a Lambda function that terminates any staging environment that's been idle for more than four hours. Should be done by end of week. That's a great idea—no more relying on humans to remember. Honestly, everything that can be automated should be automated. Humans forget. Totally agree. Preaching to the choir, that's my whole job philosophy. Okay, so the Lambda cleanup is in progress—love it. Good, what about the GPU costs? Those aren't going away. No, but I talked to our AWS rep, Vanessa, Vanessa Park. She's putting together a reserved instance proposal that could save us about thirty percent. Thirty percent? That's significant. Yeah, yeah, thirty percent is significant—that's real money. Thirty percent off the GPU specifically? Otto, what were the accessibility audit results—how many issues? Off total compute, so we'd go from like sixty-three down to forty-five, forty-six range. That's a big saving—that would basically pay for the offsite twice. When do we get the proposal? End of this week, she said. Is that typical? Like, how fast do these proposals usually come through? For reserved instances, yeah, she's been pretty responsive. Good, I like responsive vendors—makes everything easier. Hey Otto, make a note of that—Vanessa Park, reserved instances proposal, end of this week. Yeah, yeah, let's make sure we don't lose track of that one. Okay, great, what else? So, different topic, but we need to talk about the incident Thursday night. Oh right, the database thing—I got the page notification too. What happened exactly? Wait, what incident? I didn't hear about this. It was late Thursday night, so you probably wouldn't have seen it. Ugh, that one—I was hoping we could just quietly move on. No such luck, walk us through it. The connection pool on the primary Postgres instance got completely exhausted. The app was opening connections and not releasing them. Took us about forty-five minutes to figure out what was happening. The logs were not super helpful either, which made it harder to diagnose. Forty-five minutes is a long time when things are broken. Tell me about it—we were scrambling. Yeah, so that was my team. We had a missing finally block in the transaction handler, so when a transaction errored out, the connection never got released back to the pool. How many users were affected? About twelve hundred. Error rate hit eight percent during the outage. Twelve hundred? That's a lot of people. Yeah, that's not great—we need to make sure this doesn't happen again. Agreed, one is too many. It won't. The fix was straightforward once we found it—we just added the finally block. We also added a connection pool monitor that alerts if usage goes above eighty percent, so we'd catch it way before it gets critical.

Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both—onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. Brace yourselves—sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord, whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump. Twelve thousand dollars more in one month—what happened? What's driving it? Couple things. The GPU instances for the ML pipeline are expensive—that's like twenty thousand by itself now. And then somebody left three staging environments running over the weekend. That was four grand just sitting there doing nothing. Who left the staging environments up? I'm not gonna name names, but it was someone on your team, Bob. Great, I'll have a conversation with them. It happens more than you'd think—I've seen it at every company I've worked at. We can't keep doing that—four thousand dollars for a weekend. I know, I know, I'll talk to the team about it. We need to automate this, like just kill anything that's been idle. Already doing it. I'm setting up a Lambda function that terminates any staging environment that's been idle for more than four hours. Should be done by end of week. That's a great idea—no more relying on humans to remember. Honestly, everything that can be automated should be automated. Humans forget. Totally agree. Preaching to the choir, that's my whole job philosophy. Okay, so the Lambda cleanup is in progress—love it. Good, what about the GPU costs? Those aren't going away. No, but I talked to our AWS rep, Vanessa, Vanessa Park. She's putting together a reserved instance proposal that could save us about thirty percent. Thirty percent? That's significant. Yeah, yeah, thirty percent is significant—that's real money. Thirty percent off the GPU specifically? Otto, what were the accessibility audit results—how many issues? Off total compute, so we'd go from like sixty-three down to forty-five, forty-six range. That's a big saving—that would basically pay for the offsite twice. When do we get the proposal? Yeah, yeah, let's make sure we don't lose track of that one. Okay, great, what else? So, different topic, but we need to talk about the incident Thursday night. Ugh, that one—I was hoping we could just quietly move on. No such luck, walk us through it. The connection pool on the primary Postgres instance got completely exhausted. The app was opening connections and not releasing them. Took us about forty-five minutes to figure out what was happening. The logs were not super helpful either, which made it harder to diagnose. Forty-five minutes is a long time when things are broken. Tell me about it—we were scrambling. Yeah, so that was my team. We had a missing finally block in the transaction handler, so when a transaction errored out, the connection never got released back to the pool. How many users were affected? About twelve hundred. Three people for on-call is rough, that's every third night. Exactly, and the last incident was at 2 AM, people are tired. 2 AM? That's brutal. Nobody should be getting paged at 2 AM repeatedly, that's a burnout recipe. Yeah, and the person who got paged had been on call for three days straight. Can we train up more people? I can help with that. We have a good on-call training template from the infra team—takes about a day to get someone up to speed on the runbooks. That'd be great. Let's schedule it for next week. Also, David, when you do the post-mortem, make sure the runbooks get updated—half the reason on-call is stressful is because the docs are outdated. Yeah, absolutely. I'll include a runbook review as part of the post-mortem action items. Is there a way to reduce the number of incidents too, like not just better on-call but fewer pages? That's the dream. Better testing and monitoring should help over time. Prevention over firefighting, always. Actually, hey Otto, do you remember what the error rate was during Thursday's incident? I want to make sure I have the right number. It was eight percent, Bob, I mentioned it earlier. Right, eight percent. Okay, and how many users affected? Twelve hundred. Okay, let's move on to hiring. So we've got two open recs, senior backend and DevOps, both of which we desperately need. So for backend, I talked to this guy last week, Mateo, Mateo Ruiz, he's at Datadog right now. Tell, tell me about him. Really impressive—eight years of Go experience. His system design was the best I've seen in months, honestly maybe the best I've seen this year. He really gets distributed systems. Wow, that's high praise coming from you. Eight years of Go? That's a long time. Go hasn't been around that much longer than that. Yeah, he was an early adopter, moved to Go from Java and never looked back. What's the concern? Comp—he's senior staff at Datadog, which means he's probably making bank. Datadog pays well, really well. Yeah, I've heard their comp packages are wild. What's our range? 190 to 220. Is that base or total comp? Good question. Base plus equity and the usual benefits. That should be in the ballpark. Let's get him into the final round. Can you set that up this week? Yeah, I'll reach out today. Don't let him slip away—good backend people get snatched up fast. Oh, trust me, I know. I'm sending the email the second this meeting ends. What's the final round look like? Is it with you, Alice? Yeah, me and the VP of Eng, just a culture fit conversation really. The technical bar is already cleared. He's also just a really nice person, easy to talk to—I think he'd fit in well. That's good to hear. Culture matters as much as skills. On the DevOps side, I'm struggling. The last three candidates were all too junior. Like, they could manage a Terraform config but couldn't debug a Kubernetes networking issue to save their life. That's the market right now—tons of people with surface-level cloud skills. Yeah, the DevOps talent pool is tough. Where are you sourcing? LinkedIn mostly, and some recruiter pipeline. The recruiter pipeline hasn't been great for us in general. Yeah, we had a similar issue with design recruiting—the external recruiters just don't understand what we need. Maybe we need to invest in better job descriptions, really spell out what we're looking for. Have you tried the DevOps subreddit? Some really senior people hang out there. Hey Otto, who's the backend candidate again? What's his background? Actually, no, that's a good idea—I'll post there today. You could also try the CNCF Slack—lot of Kubernetes people there. Oh yeah, good call, I'll do both. What about conferences? KubeCon is coming up, right? Good point, yeah, KubeCon is in like two months, but we kinda need someone sooner than that. We could also try reaching out to people who gave talks at the last KubeCon. Those folks know their stuff. Let's try the online channels first and see what shakes loose. I'll put together a compelling post, highlight the cool problems we work on. Yeah, sell the mission—people want to work on interesting stuff. Mention the cloud spend—DevOps people love a good cost optimization challenge. Oh, totally different topic—did you guys see Prashant's Slack message about the demo Thursday? The Meridian Health thing? Yeah, they specifically want to see the reporting module, and Prashant is asking if we can show the export feature even though it's not done. Is that risky, showing something that's not ready? I mean, the UI looks fine, it's just the performance under load. I mean, it's a demo, we do this all the time. Just gotta be careful. It works, it's just slow with big datasets if the demo data is small, it'll be fine. Famous last words, the demo will be fine. Ha, I know, but seriously, with small data, it's solid. Okay, let's be smart about this—no live data, use the demo environment. Let's just use the demo environment. Carol, can you make sure the data in there looks clean? Yeah, I'll set it up. I'll make sure the sample data looks realistic but not too big. Throw some healthcare-y looking data in there—they'll appreciate that we customized it. Good idea. I'll do patient volume reports and department metrics. Also make sure the page loads fast—first impressions matter. Oh, for sure. I'll test it multiple times before Thursday. And by the way, this is a big deal—Meridian is a 200k a year opportunity. 200k, seriously? Whoa, that's our biggest prospect. Yeah, that changes the stakes a bit. No pressure on the demo or anything. Exactly, so let's make sure the demo environment is pristine. Yeah, enterprise healthcare, but they need SOC 2 compliance, which is the holdup. We've got Type 1 already, Type 2 audit starts in September. Right, but Meridian wants Type 2. Prashant is telling them Q1 next year. That's a long sales cycle. Enterprise is always long, you just have to be patient. Yeah, Q1 next year—that's like nine months from now. That's a whole pregnancy. Ha, nice analogy. Enterprise deals always are, though. Prashant's been really patient with them, he's doing a great job managing expectations. He's good at that—some salespeople would've given up by now. Yeah, enterprise sales requires a different kind of patience. Welcome to enterprise, but 200k ARR is worth waiting for. Plus the logo—having a major healthcare company as a customer opens doors. Exactly. Healthcare logos are gold for enterprise sales. Everybody wants ...to see who else in their industry is using you. Exactly, one begets many. Oh, speaking of customers, Zenith Analytics has been complaining again. Dashboard loading times—Zenith again? They've been unhappy for a while. Yeah, they've been in support tickets constantly. How big of a customer are they? Mid-tier, but they're very vocal and they refer a lot of business. That's gotta be the search thing. The new backend should help—yeah, I'll check back with them after Wednesday when the autocomplete fix goes in. Otto, what's the Meridian Health deal? How much is it worth? Good, stay on top of Zenith—they're a vocal customer and I don't want them trashing us on social media. They wouldn't do that, would they? They've done it before—there was that Twitter thread in January. I remember that, it got like 200 likes, not fun. Social media can be rough on SaaS companies—one bad thread goes viral. Alright, yeah, good. Okay, what else do we have? Hey, random thought—we should update the engineering onboarding docs. I tried to use them to onboard a contractor last month and half the links were broken. Oh yeah, same with the design docs—they reference tools we don't even use anymore. Like, there's a whole section about Sketch and we haven't used Sketch in two years. Sketch? I didn't even know we ever used Sketch. Exactly my point. The infra docs are okay because I just updated them. But the getting started guide for new engineers is super stale. Like, it still mentions Heroku. We haven't been on Heroku in over a year. Ha, wow, that's embarrassing. Can we make this a thing? Like, every quarter someone owns a docs refresh? Yeah, we could rotate it—one team per quarter. Let's do that. Bob, you're team this quarter since you brought it up. Fair enough, I walked into that one. Ha, that's what you get for having good ideas. I'll update the design section at the same time. While we're on process stuff, can we also clean up the Jira board? There are like fifty tickets in there from last year that are never gonna get done.

Three people for on-call is rough, that's every third night. Exactly, and the last incident was at 2 AM, people are tired. 2 AM? That's brutal. Nobody should be getting paged at 2 AM repeatedly, that's a burnout recipe. Yeah, and the person who got paged had been on call for three days straight. Can we train up more people? I can help with that. We have a good on-call training template from the infra team—takes about a day to get someone up to speed on the runbooks. That'd be great. Let's schedule it for next week. Also, David, when you do the post-mortem, make sure the runbooks get updated—half the reason on-call is stressful is because the docs are outdated. Yeah, absolutely. I'll include a runbook review as part of the post-mortem action items. Is there a way to reduce the number of incidents too, like not just better on-call but fewer pages? That's the dream. Better testing and monitoring should help over time. Prevention over firefighting, always. Actually, hey Otto, do you remember what the error rate was during Thursday's incident? I want to make sure I have the right number. It was eight percent, Bob, I mentioned it earlier. Right, eight percent. Okay, and how many users affected? Twelve hundred. Okay, let's move on to hiring. So we've got two open recs, senior backend and DevOps, both of which we desperately need. So for backend, I talked to this guy last week, Mateo, Mateo Ruiz, he's at Datadog right now. Tell, tell me about him. Really impressive—eight years of Go experience. His system design was the best I've seen in months, honestly maybe the best I've seen this year. He really gets distributed systems. Wow, that's high praise coming from you. Eight years of Go? That's a long time. Go hasn't been around that much longer than that. Yeah, he was an early adopter, moved to Go from Java and never looked back. What's the concern? Comp—he's senior staff at Datadog, which means he's probably making bank. Datadog pays well, really well. Yeah, I've heard their comp packages are wild. What's our range? 190 to 220. Is that base or total comp? Good question. Base plus equity and the usual benefits. That should be in the ballpark. Let's get him into the final round. Can you set that up this week? Yeah, I'll reach out today. Don't let him slip away—good backend people get snatched up fast. Oh, trust me, I know. I'm sending the email the second this meeting ends. What's the final round look like? Is it with you, Alice? Yeah, me and the VP of Eng, just a culture fit conversation really. The technical bar is already cleared. He's also just a really nice person, easy to talk to—I think he'd fit in well. That's good to hear. Culture matters as much as skills. On the DevOps side, I'm struggling. The last three candidates were all too junior. Like, they could manage a Terraform config but couldn't debug a Kubernetes networking issue to save their life. That's the market right now—tons of people with surface-level cloud skills. Yeah, the DevOps talent pool is tough. Where are you sourcing? LinkedIn mostly, and some recruiter pipeline. The recruiter pipeline hasn't been great for us in general. Yeah, we had a similar issue with design recruiting—the external recruiters just don't understand what we need. Maybe we need to invest in better job descriptions, really spell out what we're looking for. Have you tried the DevOps subreddit? Some really senior people hang out there. Hey Otto, who's the backend candidate again? What's his background? Actually, no, that's a good idea—I'll post there today. You could also try the CNCF Slack—lot of Kubernetes people there. Oh yeah, good call, I'll do both. What about conferences? KubeCon is coming up, right? Good point, yeah, KubeCon is in like two months, but we kinda need someone sooner than that. We could also try reaching out to people who gave talks at the last KubeCon. Those folks know their stuff. Let's try the online channels first and see what shakes loose. I'll put together a compelling post, highlight the cool problems we work on. Yeah, sell the mission—people want to work on interesting stuff. Mention the cloud spend—DevOps people love a good cost optimization challenge. Oh, totally different topic—did you guys see Prashant's Slack message about the demo Thursday? The Meridian Health thing? Yeah, they specifically want to see the reporting module, and Prashant is asking if we can show the export feature even though it's not done. Is that risky, showing something that's not ready? I mean, the UI looks fine, it's just the performance under load. I mean, it's a demo, we do this all the time. Just gotta be careful. It works, it's just slow with big datasets if the demo data is small, it'll be fine. Famous last words, the demo will be fine. Ha, I know, but seriously, with small data, it's solid. Okay, let's be smart about this—no live data, use the demo environment. Let's just use the demo environment. Carol, can you make sure the data in there looks clean? Yeah, I'll set it up. I'll make sure the sample data looks realistic but not too big. Throw some healthcare-y looking data in there—they'll appreciate that we customized it. Good idea. I'll do patient volume reports and department metrics. Also make sure the page loads fast—first impressions matter. Oh, for sure. I'll test it multiple times before Thursday. And by the way, this is a big deal—Meridian is a 200k a year opportunity. 200k, seriously? Whoa, that's our biggest prospect. Yeah, that changes the stakes a bit. No pressure on the demo or anything. Exactly, so let's make sure the demo environment is pristine. Yeah, enterprise healthcare, but they need SOC 2 compliance, which is the holdup. We've got Type 1 already, Type 2 audit starts in September. Right, but Meridian wants Type 2. Prashant is telling them Q1 next year. That's a long sales cycle. Enterprise is always long, you just have to be patient. Yeah, Q1 next year—that's like nine months from now. That's a whole pregnancy. Ha, nice analogy. Enterprise deals always are, though. Prashant's been really patient with them, he's doing a great job managing expectations. He's good at that—some salespeople would've given up by now. Yeah, enterprise sales requires a different kind of patience. Welcome to enterprise, but 200k ARR is worth waiting for. Plus the logo—having a major healthcare company as a customer opens doors. Exactly. Healthcare logos are gold for enterprise sales. Everybody wants ...to see who else in their industry is using you. Exactly, one begets many. Oh, speaking of customers, Zenith Analytics has been complaining again. Dashboard loading times—Zenith again? They've been unhappy for a while. Yeah, they've been in support tickets constantly. How big of a customer are they? Mid-tier, but they're very vocal and they refer a lot of business. That's gotta be the search thing. The new backend should help—yeah, I'll check back with them after Wednesday when the autocomplete fix goes in. Otto, what's the Meridian Health deal? How much is it worth? Good, stay on top of Zenith—they're a vocal customer and I don't want them trashing us on social media. They wouldn't do that, would they? They've done it before—there was that Twitter thread in January. I remember that, it got like 200 likes, not fun. Social media can be rough on SaaS companies—one bad thread goes viral. Alright, yeah, good. Okay, what else do we have? Hey, random thought—we should update the engineering onboarding docs. I tried to use them to onboard a contractor last month and half the links were broken. Oh yeah, same with the design docs—they reference tools we don't even use anymore. Like, there's a whole section about Sketch and we haven't used Sketch in two years. Sketch? I didn't even know we ever used Sketch. Exactly my point. The infra docs are okay because I just updated them. But the getting started guide for new engineers is super stale. Like, it still mentions Heroku. We haven't been on Heroku in over a year. Ha, wow, that's embarrassing. Can we make this a thing? Like, every quarter someone owns a docs refresh? Yeah, we could rotate it—one team per quarter. Let's do that. Bob, you're team this quarter since you brought it up. Fair enough, I walked into that one. Ha, that's what you get for having good ideas. I'll update the design section at the same time. While we're on process stuff, can we also clean up the Jira board? There are like fifty tickets in there from last year that are never gonna get done. Oh god, the Jira graveyard—I try not to look at it. Some of those tickets are from people who don't even work here anymore. I saw one assigned to Kevin. Kevin left like eight months ago. Yikes, okay, yeah, let's do a grooming session, but not today—schedule it for next week. I'll set it up. Thursday afternoon work for everyone? Works for me. Same. Cool, okay, the offsite—oh, oh right, the offsite. Carol, you were looking at venues. Yeah, so I looked at like eight places, and the best one by far is Summit Lodge in Lake Tahoe. Tell me about it. Oh, I love Tahoe—it's 400 a night per person, and that includes all meals and a private meeting room. Really nice place—I checked the reviews and everyone loves it. Does it have good Wi-Fi? Last offsite, the internet was terrible and we couldn't do anything. They actually advertise business-grade internet. I asked specifically, and they confirmed dedicated fiber. Dedicated fiber? Nice, that's rare for a mountain lodge. Okay, good, that was my one concern. 400 a night, and how many nights? Three nights, and we're twenty-three people now with the new hires. So that's 27,600 dollars total. That was fast math—I've been doing cloud cost calculations all week. Numbers are just flowing. Ha, cloud math brain. Well, at least you're putting it to good use. I need to grab water real quick—keep going. We'll survive without you for thirty seconds. That's within budget. What about dates? I was thinking week of June 16th—it's after the SOC 2 crunch and before summer vacations kick in. June 16th—anyone have a conflict? I love Tahoe, haven't been there in years. Beautiful place. Same, I haven't been since college actually. It's gorgeous up there. The drive from here is like four hours, right? Depends on traffic, could be three, could be five. Let me check—no, I'm good. Yeah, I'm fine. I already checked with the other teams and nobody flagged any issues. Okay, I'm back—what'd I miss? Nothing, we're just confirming dates. Okay, book it. Summit Lodge with week of June 16th, twenty-three people. Done. I'll send the invite after this call. Can we do a team dinner the first night? Like something fun, not just the hotel restaurant. I'll look into it—there are some great restaurants around the lake. Can we do some kind of team activity too, not just meetings the whole time? Definitely, last time we did that escape room thing and people loved it. I'll put together a full agenda. We can mix work sessions with fun stuff. Great. Okay, switching to roadmap. So I had a call with the advisory board last week

details.speaker: Alice
details.original: Yep. Still the number one request. Third quarter in a row.

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, what was the search latency before and after the new backend?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: p95 search latency went from 600ms to 140ms
mid_meeting_results.response: Yeah, we're looking into sharding the index better next time. That should cut it in half, easy.

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, who's fixing the autocomplete issue and when?

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, what did we decide for caching? Redis or Memcached?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Redis, because they can also use it for sessions and already have the cluster running
mid_meeting_results.response: We went with Redis for caching. It's already running for our session store, so no extra cost.

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Reindex took 9 hours. They did a shadow deployment running old and new in parallel for 2 weeks. Caught 3 bugs including a unicode/emoji edge case.

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Cards in 3 weeks, toggle for table view adds 2 more, 5 weeks total. Cards only on mobile.

mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: 14 violations, mostly contrast issues in sidebar and missing ARIA labels on modals

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, how much was the cloud bill and why did it jump?

mid_meeting_results.response: February's cloud bill was sixty-three thousand dollars. It jumped twelve thousand from last month mainly due to GPU instances for the ML pipeline and some staging environments left running over the weekend.

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, what happened in the Thursday night incident? How bad was it?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Postgres connection pool exhausted, missing finally block, 1200 users affected, 45 min outage, error rate hit 8%, PagerDuty was 12 min late
mid_meeting_results.response: The connection pool on the primary Postgres got exhausted Thursday night. About twelve hundred users affected, error rate hit eight percent for forty-five minutes.
